title: Custard tart
subtitle: Crisp shortcrust case filled with a vanilla and nutmeg egg custard
excerpt: A 23 cm custard tart: a shortcrust case blind-baked until fully golden, filled with a poured custard of eggs, cream, milk, and sugar, scented with vanilla and freshly grated nutmeg, and baked at a low temperature until the custard is just set at the edges with a wobble in the centre. Serves 6–8.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the baked custard tart chapter documents a blind-baked shortcrust case filled with a sweetened egg and cream custard flavoured with nutmeg, baked until just set. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton's baked custard section documents the slow-bake technique for egg custard, noting that a temperature that is too high causes the custard to curdle and separate. Baked custard tart with nutmeg is documented continuously in British domestic baking from the medieval period; the recipe here follows the public-domain Victorian method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The blind-baked case keeps at room temperature for 24 hours. The filled, baked tart keeps refrigerated for 2 days. Serve at room temperature — do not serve cold from the fridge.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; blind bake at 190°C fan, then reduce to 150°C fan for the custard

recipeTools.notes: 23 cm loose-bottomed fluted tart tin, 3 cm deep.
To strain the custard before pouring.

glossaryTerms.definition: The gentle, uniform jiggle of a correctly baked egg custard tart when the tin is nudged. A baked custard is done when the outer 4–5 cm is fully set and the centre still wobbles as one mass — like set jelly, not liquid. A tart that wobbles as liquid throughout is underdone. A tart that does not wobble at all, or that has a cracked surface, is overdone and will be grainy. The custard continues to cook from residual heat after it comes out of the oven.

content.text: A custard tart is judged by its texture: the custard should be smooth, creamy, and just trembling in the centre — not cracked, not grainy, not liquid. The technique is a long, slow bake at 150°C fan with the oven door left ajar, which keeps the temperature from surging and lets the custard set evenly from the outside in. The
is the only reliable cue: not time, not colour.

items.prepNote: freshly grated over the top before baking — do not use pre-ground

content.text: Make and blind bake the case

Rub together {{plain-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} until it resembles fine crumbs. Mix {{egg-yolks}} with {{water}} and add just enough to bring the dough together. Chill for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Roll to 3 mm, line a 23 cm tart tin, prick the base, blind bake with beans for 15 minutes, remove beans and bake for a further 10–12 minutes until the base is deep golden and completely dry. Cool in the tin. Reduce the oven to 150°C fan.

Whisk together the {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks, {{eggs}} whole egg, and {{caster-sugar}} until combined but not frothy. Warm the {{double-cream}} and {{whole-milk}} with the {{vanilla-bean-paste}} in a saucepan until barely simmering. Pour slowly over the egg mixture, whisking constantly. Strain through a sieve into a jug.

Place the tart tin on the oven shelf. Pour the custard into the blind-baked case — do this in the oven to avoid spilling as you carry it. Grate {{nutmeg-whole}} fresh nutmeg liberally over the surface. Bake at 150°C fan for 35–40 minutes, propping the oven door open with a wooden spoon to keep the temperature even. The tart is done when the outer 4–5 cm is set and the centre wobbles as one mass when nudged. Remove from the oven and cool in the tin for at least 1 hour before removing. Serve at room temperature, not cold.
